issues.title: Add reference implementation, test vectors
issues.author: cathieyun
issues.body:
To facilitate adoption, it would be helpful to add a reference implementation, which produces test vectors. 
The P384 ciphersuite as suggested in the spec would be sufficient for now.

issues.title: Isolate `Scalar` structure from `Group` class
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body: Currently `Scalar` methods are inside `Group`, which does not allow for an appropriate Fiat-Shamir transform. There should be a separate `ScalarField` type attribute inside `Group`, performing the scalar operations.

comments.author: cathieyun
comments.body: A bit late to this thread, but why doesn't `Scalar` being inside `Group` allow for an appropriate F-S transform? What limitation do you run into when trying to do the F-S transform with `Group.random_scalar()` functions, for instance? I'm trying to better understand the reasoning here, and why it should be propagated back to ARC (https://github.com/mmaker/spfs/pull/6#issuecomment-2713323554).

comments.author: mmaker
comments.body:
The Fiat-Shamir transform should allow to squeeze field challenges using one single algorithm, regardless of wether it's the scalar field of an elliptic curve group or not. What I meant (apologies for the quickly-typed answer) is that I can access functions that concern`Scalar` only via `Group`. 
(I was thinking that some proofs, e.g. STARK proofs, have no elliptic curve involved and so no `Group` from which one can access methods concerning `Scalar`).


Here's a cleaner argument: it would better match the specification if there was a `Scalar` class that exactly matches the `Scalar` requirements of the spec, right?

comments.author: cathieyun
comments.body: Ah the example of STARKs not having a Group to pull scalars from, is a good point and I hadn't thought of that. The current "scalar from group" is a shorthand (which may be sufficient for ARC, VOPRF, other EC-based protocols) but doesn't generalize too well.

- DLEQ proofs

- Schnorr proof of knowledge of a secret key

issues.title: Isolate commitment serialization from prove_batchable
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
The method `prove_batchable` and `verify_batchable` should be agnostic wrt the sigma protocol and the choice of the hash function. 
In order for this to happen, commitment and response serialization must be deferred to a separate procedure in SigmaProtocol.
Therefore:  move serialization to `serialize_commitment` and `serialize_response`.

issues.title: Support for short proofs
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
Right now the non-interactive sigma protocol proving function outputs batchable proofs `(commitment, response)` 
which (I think) require point validation and are generally larger than short proofs of the form `(challenge, response)`.

This issue is to track the addition of a `short_prove` and `short_verify function`

issues.title: Support messages as part of the zk proof
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
it is typical in signatures of knoweldge and sometimes in zk proofs to add a string label to a zkp, which is plugged into the fiat-shamir transformation. After a chat with trevor i have the impression that for him the way to go would be 

```
challenge = SHAKE128(description || message || commitment)
```

so i think we should find a way to add the message to the domain separator, if needed.

issues.title: Support deterministic nonce generation
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
It is good practice in the signature world to re-seed the operating system randomness with the signing key. One such example is [RFC6979](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6979). This requires some care because (1) we want to preprocess the information with the challenge generation, (2) we don't want to expose the witness to side-channel attacks, and (3) make the spec more complicated than it needs to be. 

For the case of sigma protocols, something simple as 
```
nonce     = SHAKE128(description || message || random || PAD || witness)
challenge = SHAKE128(description || message || commitment)
```

would be ideal, but the problem comes in when thinking of multi-round protocols, each of which might requires extra randomness in the middle of the protocol. 

The approach used in [Nimue](https://github.com/mmaker/nimue) is to have the NARG prover state also hold a private sponge, which is ratcheted seeded with operating system randomness every time an absorb is performed. This seems OK but wasteful.
A better alternative would be to expect the interactive (ZK) prover to take as input an rng, and seed that rng with the witness and some os randomness once and for all. The downside of this approach is that recovering the initial seed will break the whole protocol, but this does not seem to be such a problem? all in all, there is not much academic literature about leakage in zk proof of these sort.

issues.title: Keccak F-1600 does not work
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
🚨🚨🚨🚨 Currently we do not invoke the permutation function 🚨🚨🚨

https://github.com/mmaker/spfs/blob/main/poc/fiat_shamir.sage#L70

There seems to be a mismatch between python and sage bytewise operators that is leading to inconsistent results. This obviously is a major security issue and needs to be fixed.

issues.title: Add elliptic curve BLS12-381 G1 to the ciphersuite.
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
Considering as target BBS presentation proofs, @Wind4Greg requested support for BLS12-381 in the ciphersuite. 

To close this issue: 
- Add test vectors for BLS12-381 G1
- Add to the spec a description of the ciphersuite.

issues.title: Example: Linear equations for sigma protocols
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
A common extension for sigma protocols is to show that a commitment satisfies a given linear equation, without requiring expensive group operations. 

There should be an example in 
https://github.com/mmaker/draft-zkproof-sigma-protocols/blob/main/poc/test_sigma_protocols.sage that implements linear constraints in addition to morphism preimages.

An example is available at https://sigma.zkproof.org/tex-spec.pdf, section 3.3 -- but there is no need to use Gaussian elimination!  The math to work out should be pretty straightforward.

issues.title: Add elliptic curve P-384 to the ciphersuite.
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
Considering as target [ARC](https://github.com/chris-wood/draft-arc) presentation proofs, @chris-wood  requested support for P-384 within our ciphersuite. 

To close this issue: add test vectors to the specification.

comments.author: cathieyun
comments.body: FYI we are actually adding P256 to the curves for ARC, and will default to P256 for Privacy Pass. So it would be good to support both P256 (for performance) and P384, though if we have to choose between the two, I would prefer P256 since that is the choice for Privacy Pass deployment.

comments.author: mmaker
comments.body: cf. #29 -- maybe something is wrong with P384?

issues.title: Clarify scope of the specification in `README.md` and inside the specification.
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
In https://github.com/mmaker/draft-zkproof-sigma-protocols/blob/main/draft-orru-zkproof-sigma-protocols.md : 
describe what are Maurer proofs, clarify the scope is limited to those

Reminder: the scope of this specification are non-interactive, 3-round arguments for:

$Ax = B$

where $A \in \mathbb{G}^{n \times m}$ and $B \in \mathbb{G}^n$ are the public instance 
and $x \in \mathbb{F}^n$ is the private witness.

The draft is designed NOT TO step in the way of multi-round protocols or log-sized arguments.

issues.title: Add `FAQ.md` that answers the CFRG questions.
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
CFRG's wiki defines some questions which are a great starting point for the specification. 
https://wiki.ietf.org/en/group/cfrg/CFRG-Process


Add a markdown file that elaborates the following questions:

> Who in the IETF needs this solution?

List the currently-open drafts from https://datatracker.ietf.org/meeting/122/session/cfrg

> Why isn’t it solved already?

List the drafts closed. Illustrate the lack of interest from the big tech and highlight potential weaknesses in reviewing multiple different solutions in isolation. Show issues in the current BBS draft already reported.

> Is this actually an engineering problem instead of a research problem? 

This draft does not contain any research problem: these topics are well-established in research and not meant to significantly be revised in the future.

> Is the research mature enough for CFRG engagement? 

Yes; [Schnorr91] and [Maurer09] are still around.

> Scope and impact: Clarify how broad, significant, or urgent the identified cryptographic problem is.

We see this as the first step in standardizing zk proofs. List the current proposals outside the specification, the NGOs that are needing this specification, and the urgency of the identity folks in relying on a standardization approach.

issues.title: Establish ciphersuites in implementation.
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
## Cryptographic Component Combinations

Similarly to [Privacy Pass](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9497#name-ciphersuites), we must add a section in the spec and in the test vectors that ties together the different cryptographic components. I see the following components:

- **The elliptic curve**: for instance, BLS12-381
- **The hash function**: for instance, SHA-3 or TurboSHAKE
- **The codec**: the hash_to_field mechanism, rejection sampling, or modular reduction for field elements

In the Proof of Concept implementation, this requires updating the test vectors to support multiple groups and elliptic curves.

issues.title: Add template sections in Fiat-Shamir looking at other specifications
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
Check out other specification, for instance

https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-irtf-cfrg-voprf-21#name-iana-considerations-6

and add sections that are clearly missing but relevant, for instance "Security Considerations", "Post-Quantum Security", "Application Considerations"

issues.title: Statement serialiation
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
Currently, a sigma protocol is instantiated using an "initialization vector" (a label, a domain separator) of 32 bytes, and does not absorb the statement (the instance). 


## Instance 

To prove soundness (and thus stronger notions of soundness), the instance must be put in the Fiat-Shamir transformation. This has been called "strong FS" in the literature, and the case of Schnorr proofs, these generally consist of the generators used -- in the case of Maurer proofs, of the morphism used. 

The easy way out from this is to take inspiration from libsignal's statement generation process here:
https://github.com/signalapp/libsignal/blob/main/rust/poksho/src/statement.rs#L44-L88
and check in the **[latex specification](https://sigma.zkproof.org/tex-spec.pdf)** for references to scientific literature.

## Domain Separator

The way in which these 32 bytes are generated is discretionary but a standard should take a stance here and produce guidelines on how they should be generated. 

From a theoretical security standpoint, the IV should incorporate: 
- a protocol identifier, 
- a session identifier.

A way to describe how the user should pick a protocol identifier and a session identifier is desirable but not stricly needed, so for now this part can be perhaps be left to the user.

issues.title: Consider merging Fiat-Shamir spec into Sigma Protocol spec
issues.author: cathieyun
issues.body:
Given that the Fiat-Shamir spec is only used in the Sigma protocol spec, it seems like it would be simpler to combine the two and have the Fiat-Shamir definition as a sub-component of the Sigma protocol spec. (That way, we don't have to have too many layers of nested specs. For example, our spec dependencies for ARC would be: ARC -> Sigma -> F-S, and the dependences for BBS would be BBS-pseudonyms -> blinded-BBS -> Sigma -> F-S. It would be nice to remove one of those layers, but this is more of an editorial choice than a technical requirement.

Merging the F-S definitions into the Sigma Protocol spec would also simplify the requirements for F-S, as it would no longer have to work in a multi-round setting. This may allow the F-S protocol to be simpler, or at least for its security requirements to be relaxed.

issues.title: Add "Security Considerations" section
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
Fill in section "Security Considerations" currently empty. 

This section is intended to guide the reader through the different soundness and privacy
notions for sigma protocols. In particular it should have a sub-section on: 

- Security Assumptions
- Security Properties

Plus an overview of common attacks on sigma protocols, some of which can be found in https://sigma.zkproof.org/history.
Add a note on replay attacks and the meaning of the `IV` when starting the protocol.

It should also mention the UC security guarantees, and cite previous works like: 

- https://eprint.iacr.org/2022/290

issues.title: BBS and proof serialization
issues.author: BasileiosKal
issues.body:
Hey all,

There seems to be an issue when trying to use the current drafts to implement [BBS Signatures](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-irtf-cfrg-bbs-signatures/).

In the current draft, when serializing the proof, the challenge will be the first element, consistent with academic literature.

For the BBS, the challenge is at the end (see below for reasoning).

Would hate to introduce breaking changes at this point in any of the drafts. Was wondering if the function for serialising the proof could be made more general, for example, as a ciphersuite constant (different curves would need their own serialization operations either way).

The current draft could define a default one to be used with the NIST curves and one of the BBS drafts could define a BLS12-381 suite with a proof serialization that would not break the current BBS draft.


##

### BBS challenge placement explanation

A BBS proof is not just a Schnorr proof, but a set of points `(A1, A2, A3)` and then a Schnorr proof (`\pi`). In the literature this was serialised as the points first and the proof after, so it would be `BBS_proof = (A1, A2, A3, \pi)`.

If the Schnorr proof serialization convention was to be followed, the challenge would end up at the middle of the `BBS_proof`. This would make it harder to “parse” the challenge out, when multiple BBS proofs were generated in parallel (sharing the same challenge). A diversion from the literature was required and we decided to move the challenge at the end of the Schnorr proof.

issues.title: Remove mentions of operator overloading
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
Feedback from Dennis Jackson: 
> I'm not sure the draft needs to be opinionated on whether to use operator overloading or not, it's a bit of an implementation detail.

issues.title: Add more information in security sections
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body: > Some text sprinkled around the user/implementor pitfalls in the security considerations helps people appreciate the importance of standardising a thing (e.g. proof reuse, constant-time, dealing with the mod bias via the extra bytes, etc).

issues.title: Fix re-use of hash state across proofs
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
Currently the hash state of an (initialized) non-interactive proof is affected by the `prove` and `verify` functions.
https://github.com/mmaker/draft-zkproof-sigma-protocols/blob/main/poc/sigma_protocols.sage#L55

In the non-interactive proof we re-instantiate the hash state for each prove and verify, which doesn't make sense. 
https://github.com/mmaker/draft-zkproof-sigma-protocols/blob/main/poc/test_sigma_protocols.sage#L303

We should be copying the state upon proving time and proceed from there.

issues.title: Editorial comment: fix typo.
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
From @wbl: 

> I think in 2.3 for the commitment you want to map the nonces in prover_commit, not the witness. "generators" appears exactly once, which is probably a typo.

issues.title: Editorial: set expectations correctly in intro
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
From @wbl: 

> More broadly I think there introduction seems to promise a bit much.
> It discusses OR proofs, t out of n, and ultimately a degree of
> composibility (maybe), but I think a bit more needs to be said here to
> make that work. If we just want to do it for linear equations over
> prime order groups, fine, but then the intro should be revised to make
> that clear. Ideally we'd include some degree of composibility via the
> auxilliary data passed in or at least warnings about when and when not
> to compose.

issues.title: Editorial: explain where elements are tested for being in the EC subgroup
issues.author: mmaker
issues.body:
From @wbl:

> We might also need to tackle not-prime order points: verifying order is
> expensive, but there are some tricks on BLS 12 to speed it up, but
> when doing a batched proof cofactor clearing is comparatively cheap.

This is already discussed in the TeX specification -- perhaps one can just test that the instance is in the group, and not the commitment?

pulls.title: Isolate Scalar struct from Group
pulls.author: ChihChengLiang
pulls.body:
We attempt to fix #2 with this PR.

Test
```
cd poc
make
sage test_zkp.sage 
```

comments.author: mmaker
comments.body:
nice job, but there's still some things that are not handled: 

- `hash_to_scalar` is part of the description https://github.com/ChihChengLiang/spfs/blob/scalar-struct/poc/groups.sage#L190  
- the change needs to propagate to the elliptic curve implementations
In particular, https://github.com/ChihChengLiang/spfs/blob/scalar-struct/poc/groups.sage#L246 scalar serialization for NIST curves is still part of `NISTGroup`. 
Same for https://github.com/ChihChengLiang/spfs/blob/scalar-struct/poc/groups.sage#L287.

comments.author: mmaker

reviews.author: ChihChengLiang
reviews.body: I commented on the decision to use composition (wrapping GF) rather than inheritance (subclass GF)

comments.body:
Eventually I gave up the inheritance approach due to the complexity.
GF is not a simple class. It is created using a [UniqueFactory](https://doc.sagemath.org/html/en/reference//structure/sage/structure/factory.html#sage.structure.factory.UniqueFactory). To initialize it properly, we need __new__ instead of __init__, which causing a series of new complexity.
We use the `__getattr__` to get all the GF properties and methods.

pulls.title: Assemble proof test vectors (WIP)
pulls.author: cathieyun
pulls.body:
Changes:
- Update `zkp_test` to add serialized proofs to a test vector dict
- Write the test vectors to files (.json and .txt, though the .txt is currently ignored because of the .gitignore)
- Update Makefile to reflect new workflows for `make test` and `make vectors`
- Remove no-longer-relevant ARC files
- Update contributors

comments.author: mmaker
comments.body:
Thank you so much Cathie! 
Some notes:  
- The groups are instantiated at compile time, which seems useless and changes the API between what should be in the spec and what is in the POC. Ultimately, this will lead to confusion because of function signature mismatch. A better approach here I think would be to have a class factory and a ciphersuite dictionary that instantiates the ciphersuite for the sigma protocol.
- I think the name "ZKP" is overly general and misleading, we should move everything into `sigma_protocols` or `SP`.
- authorship on the Fiat--Shamir document is a bit of an over-reach (that part has not been touched, and even worse this PR introduced errors there).


I am merging this PR because we need to move fast, but these parts  will have to be changed.

pulls.title: Test vector + scaffolding for or composition
pulls.author: vishady721

comments.author: mmaker
comments.body:
Hi @vishady721 ! 
I tried to fix some inconsistencies with the function signature of the API, and the serialization function. 
More specifically, the serialization function must return bytes, and not data structures such as lists or tuples. 

I have unfortunately not managed to complete the function and there's still a small bug in the message length. 
Can you please have a look and take it from here? It should be clear what I'm trying to do. 
Feel free to revert the commitment is you want to start from scratch, but the bottom line is: keep the template functions signature

comments.author: mmaker
comments.body:
Note for later: the implementation should reject and not compile if the same witness is used in two branches of the or proof. Some notes on different ways to implement OR from a discussion with Ian Goldberg: 

```example OR statement: 

    attr1 == attr2 OR attr1 == attr3
    attr1 == attr4 + attr5

silly way: 

    attr1 == attr4 + attr5
    attr1 == attr2
    OR 
    attr1 == attr3
    attr1 == attr4 + attr5

better way: 

    instance:
    X1 = r1*A + a1*(A-3A) = morphism(),  
    X2 = r2*A + a2*B
    ..

    commit:
    SigmaProtocol(X1, X4, X5){ 
    a1'' == attr4 + attr5 
    
    morphism(r1, a1) = X1, morphism(r2, a1) = X2 
    OR
    morphism(r1', a1') = X1, morphism(r3, a1') = X3
    }

another way: 

    (attr1 - attr2)(attr1 - attr3) == 0
    attr1 == attr4 + attr5
```
I would expect `deserialize_batchable` to be the inverse of `serialize_batchable`, and that (by correctness) its output verifies. Can you pack again `challenges` with `responses`?

pulls.title: refactor(fiat-shamir): clone hash state in prove/verify instead of resetting
pulls.author: Chausseaumoine
pulls.body:
This prevents mutation of the base transcript state in `NISigmaProtocol`.
Now, `prove()` and `verify()` operate on a cloned `hash_state`, preserving
transcript determinism across multiple uses.

Also adds `.clone()` method to `KeccakDuplexSponge` using `copy.deepcopy`.

Fixes #36
